fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small utility built for the framework wiring layer. Basically we keep running into silent bugs where values get swapped when injected into methods — devs aren't noticing until runtime and it's causing real pain in production. The fix is a validation + resolution tool that checks whether a method's parameters can be safely bound before anything runs, and also lets you look up exactly what name a given parameter will be bound under.

For validation: we need to catch the case where devs only half-annotate their parameters — like the auth module approach we discussed before where some params get the name-flag and others don't. That inconsistency should be a hard reject with a structured error that tells you exactly which group is broken and who's in it.

For resolution: there's a precedence rule for figuring out the final binding name — explicit marker value wins, then falls back to the param's own name, then a default. Need to handle the edge cases (blank values, missing names, etc.) cleanly.

Should be runnable from stdin/stdout with JSON commands, and there needs to be a test harness that runs all the case files automatically. Keep the core logic clean and separate from the I/O plumbing — don't want a repeat of the last module where everything was tangled together. Output format needs to be exact, cases are in the test folder.

One extra note after the team questions came in: for the type-group rejection behavior, this is narrower than it may have sounded. A type group is only rejected when it has MORE THAN ONE parameter AND those parameters are INCONSISTENTLY marked — meaning some but not all have name_match set to true. If ALL members have name_match=true, that's accepted. If NO members have name_match=true, that's also accepted. The only conflict is the mixed case where some are true and some are false, and single-member type groups are always fine regardless of their flag. This is the same inconsistent name_match flagging scenario called out earlier where some but not all parameters in a same-typed group have name_match=true, and that exact case is what should reject with error=duplicate_parameter_type.

Also, when we do reject, the output needs to be exact and in order: 'result=rejected', then 'error=duplicate_parameter_type', then 'conflict_count=<N>' where N is the number of offending type groups, then one 'conflict=' line per offending group. Each of those lines must look like 'conflict=type:<typeLabel> positions:<pos0>,<pos1>,... names:<name0>,<name1>,...' and the positions are zero-based indices in declaration order. Important detail there: list ALL members of the ambiguous group on that line, including any that individually had name_match=true.

And on the binding-name lookup, the precedence is strict. (1) If there is NO marker at all (name_match key absent from input), the binding name is always the default 'it', regardless of what parameter_name is. (2) If a marker IS present and its value is non-blank, that explicit value is the binding name. (3) If a marker is present but its value is blank (empty string), fall back to the parameter's declared name. (4) If the marker is present with blank value AND parameter_name is null/absent, the result is null, rendered as the literal string '<null>'.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: def validate_parameter_binding(parameters: list[ParameterDescriptor]) -> ValidationResult; def resolve_binding_name(parameter_name: str | None, name_match: NameMatchMarker | None) -> str | None

trigger_keywords: how are stdout files named and organized
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness must write each case's raw stdout to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<case_index_zero_padded_3>.txt — for example rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_validate_parameter_binding@002.txt. The <cases-dir> segment is used as a namespace so different case directories never overwrite each other. The file must contain ONLY the raw stdout of the program, suitable for direct comparison against expected_output. The script is invoked as 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' and accepts '--cases-dir <subdir>' defaulting to 'public_test_cases'.

trigger_keywords: how should core logic be structured
separation between JSON parsing and business logic
where does the I/O adapter boundary sit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core validation and resolution logic must NOT know about JSON, stdin, or stdout. It must operate on plain in-memory data structures (parameter descriptions, marker objects). A separate thin adapter layer reads JSON from stdin, dispatches on the 'op' field ('validate_parameter_binding' or 'resolve_binding_name'), calls the appropriate core function, and formats the output. Errors must be reported as normalized category lines like 'error=duplicate_parameter_type' — never as raw exception messages or stack traces.

trigger_keywords: what is the default binding name when no marker
what happens with no name_match key at all
binding fallback when annotation is completely absent
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the name_match key is entirely absent from the input (not present at all, not null — just omitted), the effective binding name is always 'it'. This is the default binding name and applies regardless of what parameter_name says, even if parameter_name is a perfectly valid non-null string.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'validate_parameter_binding', 'resolve_binding_name'. Each value maps to one feature.

context_pointers.vague_reference: don't want a repeat of the last module where everything was tangled together
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The Strict Separation of Concerns NFR in the Architecture section of start.md — specifically requirement #2 stating that core validation/resolution functions must not know about JSON/stdin/wire format, enforced by a thin adapter layer

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have full clarity on right now — I'd recommend checking the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and the Architecture & Engineering Constraints section of the PRD for specifics. The exact output format and edge case behavior should be derivable from those artifacts.

feature1_validate_parameter_binding.json.description: Validate that a method-style parameter list is unambiguous for value binding. A parameter list is ambiguous when two or more parameters share the same declared type but only SOME (not all, and not none) of those same-typed parameters are flagged to be bound by name. A list is accepted when every group of same-typed parameters is either fully name-flagged or fully unflagged, or when all parameter types are distinct. Each input is an ordered list of parameters; each parameter has a positional name, a type label (parameters sharing a label share a type), and a boolean name_match flag. Accepted lists report conflict_count=0. Rejected lists report the normalized error category and one conflict line per offending type, listing that type's label, the zero-based positions of the conflicting parameters, and their names.

cases.expected_output:
result=rejected
error=duplicate_parameter_type
conflict_count=1
conflict=type:String positions:0,1 names:a,b

result=rejected
error=duplicate_parameter_type
conflict_count=1
conflict=type:String positions:0,1,2 names:a,b,c

feature2_resolve_binding_name.json.description: Resolve the effective binding name for a single parameter from its declared parameter name and an optional name-match marker. Rules: if no marker is present, the binding falls back to the default binding name 'it', regardless of the parameter name. If a marker is present and carries a non-blank explicit value, that explicit value becomes the binding name. If a marker is present but its value is blank, the binding name is the parameter's own name; and if the parameter name itself is absent (null), the binding resolves to null. The input carries an optional parameter_name (null when absent) and an optional name_match object holding the marker's value (omitted entirely when there is no marker). Output is a single binding line; a null result is rendered as the literal placeholder <null>.
